coredata.dc:title: (B, N)-codoped carbon dots for sensitive luteolin detection and HepG2 cell imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yongqiang
Hou, Xingyu
Li, Yali
Sun, Xue
Hu, Rui
Lv, Yuguang
Jia, Rui
Ding, Lixin
coredata.dc:description: Luteolin (3′,4′,5,7-tetrahydroxyflavone, LTL) is a kind of hydrophobic flavonoid, which has a number of pharmacological actions. It has a number of pharmacological actions, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, anticancer, and antihepatic fibrosis properties. High-dose LTL, on the other hand, can cause oxidative DNA damage and hydroxyl radical generation. It is critical to create easy, sensitive, and dependable LTL detection technologies and delivery methods for LTL quality control, clinical drug monitoring and enhancing drug bioavailability. In this work, a novel phenylboronic acid derived quantum dots (P-QDs) were synthesized for LTL sensitive detection and pH-responsive delivery. It showed remarkable selectivity and a brilliant fluorescence with a quantum yield of 43.62 %. In vitro experiments reveal negligible cytotoxicity. P-QDs and LTL coupling (P-QDs/LTL) showed powerful proliferation inhibition and migration restriction effects on HepG2 cells. In addition, P-QDs/LTL could time-dependently release it to restore fluorescence in the microacidic environment of HepG2 cells, thus demonstrating satisfactory fluorescence imaging capability. This strategy demonstrated a favourable linear relationship between LTL concentration and the fluorescence intensity of P-QDs in the range of 0.02–200 μM (R2 = 0.9952), with a detection limit of 1.16 nM (S/N=3). The recoveries in different real samples were in the range of 85.23 %-108.04 % with RSDs ≤6.1 %. The material is both a new attempt at LTL detection methodology and has significant promise for providing pH-responsive intracellular release of drug monitoring and cancer therapies from naked QDs without the requirement for sophisticated modified grafting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Boronate affinity
Luteolin
Detection
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the P-QDs and LTL system
Selectivity and stability of the P-QDs fluorescent sensors
Intracellular imaging and drug release monitoring
Migration inhibition of HepG2 cells by P-QDs/LTL

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a) and particle size distribution (b) of the P-QDs. FTIR spectrum of P-QDs and P-QDs/LTL (c), XRD diffraction pattern (d). XPS survey spectrum (e), C1s (f), N1s (g), B1s (h) and O1s (i) of P-QDs.
P-QDs fluorescence emission spectra in the presence of various concentrations of LTL (0.02–200 μM) (a) and linear calibration chart (λEx = 366 nm) (b).
Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectra (a), P-QDs and P-QDs/LTL fluorescence lifetimes (b).

Effects of pH on the detection of LTL (a), Effects of incubation time on the fluorescence intensities of the P-QDs aqueous solution (b), Effects of temperature on P-QDs detection of LTL(c). F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of the P-QDs in the absence and presence of LTL, respectively.
Bar chart diagram of selectivity for P-QDs against different competing molecules.
Cell viability of P-QDs, LTL and P-QDs/LTL on HepG2 cell line.
Fluorescence images of HepG2 cells incubated with 90 μg mL−1 P-QDs/LTL system for 0.5 h (a, b, c) 2 h (d, e, f), and 4 h (g, h, i).
Effect of P-QDs, P-QDs/LTL, and LTL on the migratory potential of HepG2 cells in vitro.
Scheme of the preparation of P-QDs and the detection of LTL.
Recovery of LTL in three samples by P-QDs assay.
Comparison of analytical performances for the determination of LTL.

head.ce:title: (B, N)-codoped carbon dots for sensitive luteolin detection and HepG2 cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, Jiamusi 154007, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, Jiamusi 154007, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Harbin Customs Technology Centre, Jiamusi 154007, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, Jiamusi 154007, China (Y. Lv).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yongqiang Wang and Xingyu Hou contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luteolin (3′,4′,5,7-tetrahydroxyflavone, LTL) is a kind of hydrophobic flavonoid, which has a number of pharmacological actions. It has a number of pharmacological actions, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, anticancer, and antihepatic fibrosis properties. High-dose LTL, on the other hand, can cause oxidative DNA damage and hydroxyl radical generation. It is critical to create easy, sensitive, and dependable LTL detection technologies and delivery methods for LTL quality control, clinical drug monitoring and enhancing drug bioavailability. In this work, a novel phenylboronic acid derived quantum dots (P-QDs) were synthesized for LTL sensitive detection and pH-responsive delivery. It showed remarkable selectivity and a brilliant fluorescence with a quantum yield of 43.62 %. In vitro experiments reveal negligible cytotoxicity. P-QDs and LTL coupling (P-QDs/LTL) showed powerful proliferation inhibition and migration restriction effects on HepG2 cells. In addition, P-QDs/LTL could time-dependently release it to restore fluorescence in the microacidic environment of HepG2 cells, thus demonstrating satisfactory fluorescence imaging capability. This strategy demonstrated a favourable linear relationship between LTL concentration and the fluorescence intensity of P-QDs in the range of 0.02–200 μM (R2 = 0.9952), with a detection limit of 1.16 nM (S/N=3). The recoveries in different real samples were in the range of 85.23 %-108.04 % with RSDs ≤6.1 %. The material is both a new attempt at LTL detection methodology and has significant promise for providing pH-responsive intracellular release of drug monitoring and cancer therapies from naked QDs without the requirement for sophisticated modified grafting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Luteolin (3′,4′,5,7-tetrahydroxyflavone, LTL) is a typical bioactive flavonoid compound extensively found in plants, vegetables and medicinal herbs [1]. It possesses a variety of pharmacological effects, including anti-cancer, anti-liver fibrosis, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant and anti-diabetic properties [2–5]. Particularly, medical circles are paying more attention to LTL for its intriguing hepatoprotective benefits, such as the therapy of metabolism-associated fatty liver, hepatic fibrosis, activation of apoptosis in hepatocellular cancer cells, and modulation of lipid homeostasis [6]. Nonetheless, larger levels of LTL are pro-oxidants, promoting oxidative damage to DNA while increasing hydroxyl radical generation [7]. Considering the extensive pharmacological effects and potential biological toxicity of LTL, developing a simple, sensitive, and reliable LTL determination method is crucial for drug quality control and clinical drug monitoring.

Several strategies, including ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry, capillary electrophoresis, and high performance liquid chromatography, have been utilized for LTL measurements [8,9]. Due to the low sensitivity and high instrument cost of sample pre-treatment operations, the application of these methods in real samples is subjected to limitation [10,11]. In comparison with the above methods, fluorescence analysis has become an indispensable analytical technique by virtue of its ultra-sensitivity, rapidity, simplicity, wide linear range and low detection limits [12]. Notably, nanofluorescent materials with excellent fluorescence quality can not only be employed as detection probes for a wide range of pharmaceuticals but also as delivery carriers for various drugs with low bioavailability, indicating a promising prospects in the medical industry [13]. Among various nanostructured materials, fluorescent quantum dots (QDs) have evolved into one of the most revolutionary nanomaterials for drug detection and delivery due to their attractive optical properties and wide range of applications in bioimaging and nanotherapeutics [14,15]. It is a zero-dimensional semiconductor nanocrystal with a minimal size, favourable biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and outstanding photostability. Currently, a number of QDs-based fluorescent probes have been designed for the quantitative detection and delivery of flavonoids. For instance, Sun et al. designed a QDs with a fluorescence emission that was sharply burst in the range of 0.23–50.0 μM after the addition of LTL [16]. Wen et al. prepared QDs loaded with curcumin from green tea, which significantly improved the solubility of curcumin [17]. Unfortunately, although this design increases the pharmacokinetic characteristics and biofavorability of the loaded drug, it is incapable of delivering the drug in a targeted manner and is potentially harmful to normal cells. Smart nanodrug delivery systems that are particularly sensitive to specific tumour microenvironments need to be developed immediately [18].

Due to the limited water solubility of LTL, its oral bioavailability is only 17.5 % [19]. Therefore, taking advantage of its cis-diol structure to develop a fluorescent material for the detection and delivery of LTL is undoubtedly a two-for-one solution. Reportedly, phenylboronic acid (PBA) selectively recognizes cis-diol molecules.Under alkaline conditions, the cis-dihydroxyl group forms a five- or six-membered cyclic ester with the PBA's tetrahedral boron ion (sp3 hybridisation), and under acidic conditions, the boron atoms change from sp3 hybridisation to sp2 hybridisation, resulting in the dissociation of the formed covalent bonds to reversibly open rings [18]. Owing to their substantial sugar metabolism, the majority of cancer cells are acidic. PBA can be used as a cis-diol drug sensor as well as a ph-responsive drug delivery vehicle [20,21]. For targeted medication delivery, nanoparticles frequently need their surfaces modified. However, the larger particle size of functionalized QDs not only affects transmission efficiency, but also affects system stability, and the degradation of the grafted material in cells can be potentially hazardous [22]. If unmodified bare QDs can be designed as responsive drug delivery systems, the complex modification methods would be avoided, benefiting the development of drug delivery systems.

The development of bare QDs based on boronate affinity for simultaneous drug detection and administration has received little attention. In this study, we used a simple hydrothermal technique to create P-QDs from 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid (CPBA) and 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) as the basic components. It is a reliable and efficient LTL sensor that meets the requirements of high selectivity and reliable quantification, and it has exhibited excellent outcomes in three real samples. Furthermore, the nanomaterials have remarkable cytocompatibility and cell imaging properties. In the slightly acidic environment of cancer cells, the borate ring formed by P-QDs and LTL disconnects and easily releases the hydrophobic anticancer drugs, demonstrating extraordinary cancer suppressive efficacy.

CPBA and APBA (97 %) were obtained from Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd. (Ward Hill, MA, USA). Ferrous chloride (FeCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassiumchloride(KCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), cobaltous chloride (CoCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), maltose, sucrose, carbodiimide (EDC, ≥98 %), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98 %), 5,7-dihydroxy chromone, LTL, Chlorogenic acid, Resveratrol, Kaempferol, and Morin were all purchased from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffered solution (PBS) was purchased from Lonza (Shanghai, China). Sulfuricacid(H2SO4) and quinine sulfate (98 %) were obtained from Sigma (New York, NY, USA). Human hepatocellular carcinoma cells (HepG2) was purchased from Korean Cell Line Bank (Seoul, Korea), CCK8 assay was conducted using the CCK8 kit (Dojindo, Shanghai, China). Every experimental procedures were accepted by the Biological and Medical Ethics Committee of Jiamusi University (License number: JMSU-2024083002).

UV–Vis Analysis UV–visible spectra were recorded by spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2550, Kyoto, Japan). The relative fluorescence was performed on the 970 CRT Fluorescence Spectrophotometer with Ex/Em slits of 10.0/10.0 nm (Shanghai, Sanke Instrument Co., Ltd). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin (FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA) microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a IRTracer-100 Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Chemical composition was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Phi V5000). Transmission electron microscopy was carried out using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The structures were characterized by using an X ray diffract meter (XRD, XRD-6000, Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). The pH values of solutions used in the experiment were measured using a PHS-3F pH meter (Shanghai Yidian Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd). Fluorescence imaging experiments were performed under a fluorescence microscope (Nikon Instruments Co., Ltd., China). The luminescence was measured using a SUNRISE Microplate Reader (Tecan, Switzerland).

The synthesis of P-QDs was based on a previously reported method, but with minor modifications [23]. Briefly, 0.50 g APBA and 0.85 g CPBA were added to 20 mL of water, sonicated for 10 min, and then placed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacted in an oven at 192 °C for 8.4 h. After reaching room temperature, filtering with a microporous membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm is performed to remove precipitates. To get rid of minor impurities and unreacted source components, the product was dialyzed with water (Mw 1000) for 48 h. The purified brown solution was dried under vacuum at 70 °C for 4 h and the powder formed was P-QDs. The P-QDs preparation process and LTL detection are shown in Scheme 1 .

According to a predetermined process, the QY of P-QDs was determined. In a nutshell, the standard was chosen to be quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY: 0.54 at 365 nm) [24]. Eq. (1) was used to compute the fluorescence quantum yield (Φfl ):
(1)
Φ
fl
=
Φ s
×
F /
F s
×
A s
/ A
×
n /
n s
2
where Φs is the reported quantum yield of the standard, F and Fs are the integrated areas of the sample and the standard solutions, respectively; A and As are the absorbances of the sample and the standard solutions, respectively, and n is the refractive index, with 1.33 serving as the default for both quinine sulfate and P-QDs.

Probes for the detection of LTL were prepared by diluting the prepared P-QDs with deionised water into a solution. 1.0 mL each of diluted P-QDs, PBS buffer solution at pH 8.5, and LTL solution of known concentration were prepared into a mixture and incubated for 5 min, and then the fluorescence intensity was detected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 366 nm. The procedure for the blank sample was the same as above, except that the LTL solution was replaced with the same volume of pure water, and the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample was marked as F0 .

Human hepatocellular carcinoma cells (HepG2) were cultured in DMEM culture medium containing 10 % foetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % streptomycin/penicillin. Cells were kept in an incubator containing 5 % carbon dioxide at 37 °C throughout the experiments.

The CCK-8 kit was used to detect the cell viability of P-QDs. Briefly, HepG2 cells (5 × 103 per well) were inoculated in 96-well plates, and five wells were done in each set of replicates. After incubation at 37°C and 5 % CO2 for 24 h, the growth medium was replaced with fresh DMEM containing different concentrations of P-QDs (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, and 140 μg mL−1) for another 24 h. The cells were then washed with PBS. The cells were then washed with PBS, and 10 μL of CCK-8 and 90 μL of DMEM solution containing 10 % foetal bovine serum were added to each well. Incubation was then continued at 37°C and 5 % CO2 for 4 h. Finally, the absorbance of each well was detected using an enzyme marker at an emission wavelength of 450 nm. The cell viability was calculated using the Eq. (2). The experiment was repeated three times independently.
(2)
Cell viability
%
=
O
D
sample
- O
D
blank
O
D
control
- O
D
blank
× 100 %

For cell imaging studies, HepG2 cells were propagated in culture media (DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % foetal bovine serum) at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. After the cells were attached to the wall, the original medium was replaced with culture medium containing DMEM with 90 μg mL−1 P-QDs/LTL. The procedure for the configuration of P-QDs/LTL-containing culture medium was as follows: 0.5 mL of double antibody, 0.5 mL of glutamine and 5 mL of foetal bovine serum were added to 44.0 mL of DMEM medium under aseptic conditions, and after thorough mixing, 4.5 mg of P-QDs/LTL was added to it, which was dissolved by ultrasonication and then filtered aseptically, and stored in a sealed container. The cells were washed three times with PBS at 0.5, 2, and 4 h, respectively, and finally, the cells were imaged by a fluorescence microscope.

The ability of the P-QDs, LTL, and P-QDs/LTL systems to inhibit the migration of HepG2 cells was evaluated using an in vitro scratch assay, in which HepG2 cells (1.5 × 10−5 cells/well) were inoculated in 12-well plates. After 24 h of growth, a scratch operation was performed with a 200 μL pipette tip in each well, followed by two washes with PBS to remove the isolated cells. Then, 150 μL of fresh culture medium and P-QDs, LTL, P-QDs/LTL conjugates at the final IC50 concentration were added and incubated for 24 h. Cells containing only culture medium were used as a control, and the positive control was fresh culture medium containing 10 % foetal bovine serum. Each concentration was repeated three times. Scratch growth was monitored at 0 and 12h under a fluorescence microscope. Scratch images were analysed using ImageJ, and the scratch area was calculated. The migration ratios of the cells were calculated using Eq. (3):
(3)
Migration ratios
%
=
A
t 0
-
A
Δ t
A
t 0
× 100 %
where At0 is the area of wound measured immediately after scratching and AΔt is the area of wound measured t h postscratching [25].

Dried Lonicera japonica Thunb, celery and Nelumbo nucifera Gaertn were separately crushed into chips and finely weighed at 5.0 g. The extract was refluxed with 20 mL of 85 % v/v ethanol solution for 4 h [26]. Subsequently, the extract was centrifuged at 4000 r min−1 for 20 min, and the sample solution was prepared by transferring 10 mL of the supernatant to a 100 mL volumetric flask, which was diluted with 0.1 M PBS (pH 8.5) and fixed to volume. The recovery experiments were carried out by adding the appropriate amount of LTL to the sample using the standard addition method.

Extensive morphological and property studies of the prepared QDs were carried out using a variety of spectroscopic and transmission electron microscopes (TEM). P-QDs were irregularly spherical, with a planar lattice spacing of 0.22 nm in the graphite nucleus of the carbon atoms (Fig. 1 a), which were consistent with the findings of Sun et al [27]. Most of the fluorescent nanoparticles were about 2.8–4.0 nm in size (Fig. 1b). The FT-IR spectrum was shown in the Fig. 1c. The clear broad band at 3000–3500 cm−1 could be attributed to NH2 [28]. The peak at 2930 cm−1 is CH stretching vibration [29]. The peak at 1700 cm−1 is attributed to CO [30]. The peak at 1420 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the COH [31]. Two distinct absorption bands at 1190 cm−1 and 1020 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibration and stretching vibration of BOH, respectively [32]. The reduction of the characteristic BOH peak upon binding to LTL indicates the involvement of the boron group in the process of ring formation with the cis-diol. The peak at 1080 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the CB bond [31]. The peaks at 810 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the aromatic CN [28]. The peak at 710 cm−1 can be attributed to the CCC binding vibration [33]. This suggested that the P-QDs were synthesized successfully.

As shown in Fig. 1d, the X-ray diffractogram of P-QDs has two distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ of 24.8 and 28.2, which reveals its general carbon nature and confirms the successful doping of B [34]. The elemental composition of the surface of P-QDs was analysed by XPS. The contents (%) of C, N, O, and B on the surface of P-QDs were 58.79, 4.6, 18.63, and 17.91 %, respectively, and the binding energies of the elements C1s, N1s, O1s, and B1s were 284.82, 399.91, 532.71, and 191.77 eV, respectively (Fig. 1e). The C1s energy level can be split into three peaks: 284.8, 285.4 and 288.7 eV. These peaks can be attributed to CC, CN and NCN, respectively [35,36] (Fig. 1f). The N1s energy spectrum binding energy is 399.4 eV, which can be attributed to CN [37] (Fig. 1g). The BOH binding energy in the B1s energy spectrum is 191.42 eV [38] (Fig. 1h). The bands at 533.02 and 534.9 eV in the O1S high-resolution XPS spectrum indicate the presence of BO and CO groups, respectively [36,38] (Fig. 1i). This result validates the successful synthesis of P-QDs by hydrothermal methods.

We investigated the fluorescence response of LTL to P-QDs. The fluorescence of P-QDs burst when LTL was added to the solution. Fig. 2 a shows the fluorescence spectra of P-QDs after adding different concentrations of LTL. As the concentration of LTL increased from 0.02–200 μM, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased and showed a good linear relationship. The linear equation was F/F0 = −0.19901 Lg (CLTL) + 0.64589 (R2 = 0.9952), and the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.16 nM, which was calculated by 3 s/k (s was the standard deviation of the blank solution and k is the absolute value of the slope of the calibration curve) (Fig. 2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the P-QDs and LTL system
ce:section.ce:para:
UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy are powerful tools to verify the fluorescence properties and quenching mechanisms of P-QDs [39].The UV–visible spectra demonstrated distinct absorption bands for the quencher at 261 nm and 350 nm (Fig. 3 a). The peak at 261 nm is usually attributed to the n → π* electron leaps of CN and CO [40]. The peak at 350 nm can be attributed to the n-p* jump [41]. The absorption spectrum of LTL at 350 nm does not overlap with the Em (emission) spectrum of P-QDs, so the possibility of a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism during quenching was ruled out. To further explain whether the mechanism by which LTL bursts P-QDs fluorescence is photo-induced electron transfer (PET) or internal filtering effect (IFE), we also calculated the bursting constants using the Stern-Volmer equation in Eq. (4):
(4)
F 0
F
=
k q
τ 0
C LTL
+ 1
where F0 and F are the FL values of the P-QDs in the absence and presence of LTL, respectively; and CLTL is the concentration of LTL (μM), kq is quenching rate, and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime in absence of LTL, 6.12 ns.

By calculating the average fluorescence lifetimes of P-QDs and P-QDs/LTL, we found that the average fluorescence lifetimes of P-QDs in the presence and absence of LTL were 6.12 ns, and those of P-QDs/LTL were 6.04 ns (Fig. 3 b), with no detailed changes in the two, which suggests that the quenching of P-QDs by LTL is neither a FRET nor PET principle. Therefore, we identified the quenching mechanism as IFE.

We optimised the important parameters such as synthesis time, dispersion medium synthesis temperature, and CPBA/APBA ratio. Controlling the synthesis temperature is critical to achieving optimal QD fluorescence performance. The fluorescence intensity was decreased when the reaction was at 80 °C (Fig. S1a). This may be attributed to the inability of the lower temperature to cause surface defects in the reaction feedstock or the low binding efficiency of CPBA to APBA. The fluorescence intensity of the P-QDs was satisfactory at a reaction temperature of 160 °C, but it gradually decreased as the temperature increased, which could be attributed to the higher temperature destroying the structure of the P-QDs or reducing the functional groups on their surfaces. The fluorescence intensity of the product reached its peak when the reaction time was 10 h (Fig. S1b). Therefore, the time in the subsequent reaction was set to 10 h. The best fluorescence effect of the synthetic material was obtained when the amount of substances of APBA and CPBA was 1:1, and the amount of the two raw materials was retained at the above ratio during the one-factor optimisation (Fig. S1c).

Box-Behnken design was employed to obtain the optimum conditions for the preparation of P-QDs. The independent variable conditions for obtaining maximum fluorescence intensity were as follows: reaction time: 8.4 h, ratio of the amount of substances of APBA and CPBA: 1:1.4, and reaction temperature: 192 °C (Table S2 and Fig. S2).

It has been shown that quenching efficiency is also affected by incubation time [31]. The acidity and basicity of dispersion media have a significant impact on the fluorescence properties of sensing probes [42]. Therefore, the effect of P-QDs in BPS buffer solution with different pH on the sensing performance of LTL was studied. The fluorescence intensity of P-QDs/LTL system decreases slowly when the pH increases from 5.5 to 6.5, which can be explained that the hybridization mode of phenylboronic acid on the probe surface has not changed significantly under acidic conditions (Fig. 4 a and Fig. S3). When the pH value of PBS buffer changed from 6.5 to 7.5, the fluorescence was significantly quenched, which could be explained as the formation of ester ring between phenylboronic acid and the cis-diol structure of LTL on the probe surface. When the pH value of PBS buffer increased to 8.5, the fluorescence was quenched to the maximum extent, that is to say, the probe and LTL had the best combination at pH 8.5. The fluorescence intensity of P-QDs rapidly drops within 5 min, as seen in Fig. 4b, and then does not significantly vary with increasing time, suggesting that LTL detection can be finished quickly. P-QDs had the best relative fluorescence intensity when the detection ambient temperature was 25 °C. The results indicated that the sensor could detect LTL at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the P-QDs fluorescent sensors
ce:section.ce:para: We chose common glycoconjugates (sucrose, maltose), common ions in the physiological environment and polycyclic compounds (chlorogenic acid, 5,7-dihydroxy chromone, chlorogenic acid and resveratrol) to evaluate the selectivity of P-QDs accordingly. Fig. 5 shows that none of the molecules or ions, except LTL, had a significant effect on the fluorescence properties of P-QDs. Since the remaining analogues do not have a cis-diol structural unit, they are unable to cross-link with the probe to form an assembly, resulting in a corresponding insignificant change in fluorescence [31]. Therefore, P-QDs can satisfy the quantitative analysis of the detected substances.

To test the stability of P-QDs at high ionic strengths, we employed a series of NaCl solutions with concentrations ranging from 0–1.0 M. The NaCl solutions were used to test the stability of P-QDs at high ionic strengths. The results were shown in Fig. S4a, where the fluorescence intensity tended to be stable even at a NaCl concentration of 1.0 M. Furthermore, after 6 weeks in a room temperature context, the fluorescence intensity of the nanomaterials reduced by only 2.42 % (Fig. S4b), indicating that this remarkable fluorescence stability not only allowed for the detection of LTL, but also allowed for the possibility of cellular imaging.

To confirm the biocompatibility of the fluorescent probe and the cytotoxicity of the probe and LTL system at different concentrations, the cytotoxicity of P-QDs and P-QDs/LTL on HepG2 cells was compared using the CCK-8 assay. The P-QDs showed favourable biocompatibility, with the cell survival rate still greater than 82 % at concentrations as high as 140 μg mL−1 (Fig. 6 ). The survival rate of HepG2 cells incubated with the addition of P-QDs/LTL, the survival rate of HepG2 cells was lower than that of cells incubated with free LTL and P-QDs, which may be attributed to the good biocompatibility and ultra-small particle size of P-QDs, which can bind with hydrophobic LTL to form a system, thus increasing the amount of LTL entering the cells and thus exerting therapeutic effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular imaging and drug release monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the performance of nanoprobes in in vitro intracellular imaging and drug tracer release sensing, we used fluorescence microscopy to observe the changes in fluorescence signals of HpeG2 cells incubated with P-QDs/LTL over an extended period of time (Fig. 7 ). Before taking fluorescence images, the adherent cells were washed six times with PBS solution to remove unbound nanoprobes. A small amount of blue fluorescence was observed in the cells at 0.5 h of incubation, which can be attributed to the fact that part of the P-QDs/LTL system had entered the cells and released some of the LTL molecules into the cytoplasm in the slightly acidic environment of the cancer cells. A significant increase in fluorescence intensity was observed in the cytoplasm at 2 h, proving that LTL was further released, and after 4 h of incubation, the fluorescence intensity was observed to reach the limit, proving that LTL was completely released, indicating that the P-QDs/LTL system could be effectively internalised into HepG2 cells, suggesting that this system has the potential for visual drug tracking.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Migration inhibition of HepG2 cells by P-QDs/LTL
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 shows the statistical analysis of the in vitro scratch assay, and the results showed that both the P-QDs/LTL system and LTL inhibited the migration of HepG2 cells, while only P-QDs had the narrowest scratch after 12 h of incubation. The migration rates of HepG2 cells after 12 h of incubation in the P-QDs, LTL, and P-QDs/LTL systems were 74.14, 59.48 and 12.07 %, respectively. Compared with the control group, the P-QDs/LTL system at IC50 concentration showed significant migration inhibition of HepG2 cells that may be the fact ester rings formed under the slightly acidic conditions when the P-QDs/LTL enter the cancer cells. Afterwards the boron atoms varying from sp3 to sp2 hybridisation make the dissociation of the covalent bonds formed and result in the release of LTL, which then exerts the viability and multiplication of the cells. These results indicated that P-QDs/LTL had the potential to inhibit HepG2 cell migration.

We compared the analytical methods for LTL detection with those previously reported in the literature. For LTL detection, the present analytical parameters are comparable or better than most of the reported fluorescence analysis methods (Table 2). Among them, the LTL electrochemical sensor with nanocomposite-modified glassy carbon electrodes developed by Tang et al. could only detect 0.01–10 μM, whereas the detection range of the P-QDs was in the range of 0.02–200 μM. This result suggested that the material not only rivaled the lower detection limits of many electrochemical sensors, but also greatly expanded the range of fluorescent materials for the detection of LTL. Compared with the rest of the LTL probe structures, the structure of P-QDs was characterised by one-step synthesis, assembly and modification in a single step without the need for stepwise grafting, as in the case of the probe synthesised by Shen et al. which required two parts and was relatively cumbersome [45]. Therefore, our strategy is explore a reliable detection method with excellent characteristics such as high sensitivity, simple preparation and wide linear range. Comparative results shows that the proposed method has better analytical parameters and potential for practical applications.

In order to evaluate the validity of the proposed method, the P-QDs for the determination of LTL in real samples was tested by measuring the LTL concentration in the Lonicera japonica Thunb, celery, Nelumbo nucifera Gaertn samples. The samples were analyzed using the standard addition method. The results were shown in Table 1
, and the spiked recoveries were in the range of 85.23–108.04 % with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 6.1 %, which verified the practical application of the method.

In conclusion, phenylboronic acid-functionalized P-QDs were synthesised with exceptionally tiny particle sizes, acceptable optical characteristics, and favourable biocompatibility using a straightforward, budget-friendly, one-step hydrothermal approach. Its application as a fluorescence sensor enables us to detect LTL with high sensitivity. The wavelengths at which the sensor was excited and released were 366 nm and 432 nm, respectively. Compared to other competitors, the P-QDs showed better LTL selectivity and sensitivity. The linear range was wide (0.02–200 μM), and LOD was 1.16 μ M. Furthermore, P-QDs/LTL could effectively internalize into HepG2 cells for fluorescence imaging in vitro. The probe's potential to regain P-QD fluorescence following quencher release enabled the monitoring of intracellular drug release. Simultaneously, the probe showed a significant reduction in the activity of cancer cells. To sum up, P-QDs/LTL presents a new approach to the highly sensitive, extremely accurate, and selective evaluation of LTL and holds great potential for application in cancer imaging and treatment.

Yongqiang Wang: Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xingyu Hou: Visualization, Software, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Yali Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Software. Xue Sun: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software. Rui Hu: Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology. Yuguang Lv: Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Rui Jia: Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology. Lixin Ding: Validation, Software, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Rui Jia reports financial support was provided by Heilongjiang Provincial Department of Education. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Basic scientific research business of Heilongjiang Province (2022-KYYWF-0614).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111562.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
